questions.passage: The local festival is often advertised as proof that a community still has a shared life. Streets close, food stalls appear, children carry paper flags, and residents who rarely speak find themselves watching the same parade. The scene can be genuinely moving. For a few hours, the town becomes visible to itself, not as a tax district or a set of property lines, but as a crowd with a rhythm. Yet festivals can flatter a community as easily as they can reveal it. Public celebration depends on simplification. The town must choose symbols, songs, costumes, and stories that can be repeated without lengthy explanation. Such simplification is not automatically false. A community cannot gather around every disagreement at once. But the festival's cheerful compression can also hide exclusions. The official food may belong to one ethnic group while others are invited only as spectators. The historical pageant may end before labor conflict, displacement, or segregation enters the story. Belonging is performed, but the script may have been written by people who already belonged. Critics sometimes respond by dismissing festivals as nostalgia or civic theater. That dismissal is too easy. Theater is not the opposite of truth; it is one way societies make truth visible, selective, and debatable. A parade that includes contested memories, new residents, and even awkward acknowledgments of past harm may produce a stronger belonging than one that offers only polish. The issue is not whether public identity is staged, but whether the staging leaves room for revision. The crowd itself is morally ambiguous. It can make a shy resident feel accompanied; it can also pressure dissenters to smile. It can turn strangers into neighbors for an afternoon; it can also convert individuality into costume. The same chant that warms one person may warn another that refusal will be noticed. Festivals therefore test whether communal joy can remain hospitable to partial belonging, to those who participate without surrendering every reservation. A good festival does not prove that the community is unified. It gives the community a public occasion to ask what unity would require. Its value lies not in making conflict disappear, but in making shared space desirable enough that conflict might be faced there rather than avoided elsewhere. The strongest festivals admit this tension without becoming lectures. They leave room for pleasure while refusing to pretend that pleasure settles every dispute. A parade can carry a contested history honestly if it allows more than one group to recognize itself in the route, the music, and the stories told afterward. Food stalls can be commercial and still reveal who has been invited to sell, cook, and name the local. The danger is not simplification itself; every shared ritual simplifies. The danger is simplification that congratulates the majority for tolerating the presence of others without letting those others shape the celebration. A festival worth defending is not one in which everyone feels the same pride. It is one in which the public form is generous enough for disagreement to appear without being treated as a spoiling intrusion. A festival that can tolerate such complexity may feel less seamless, but that loss of seamlessness is not necessarily a failure. It may be the sign that the public form has become capacious enough to hold more than one memory at once. The question is not whether every booth, song, or procession must carry a footnote. Celebration would collapse under that demand. The question is whether the official story leaves openings through which those outside its first version can enter as authors rather than decorations. When it does, the festival becomes more than a pleasant interruption of ordinary life. It becomes a rehearsal for a community still learning how to describe itself.
questions.stem: The passage’s main argument about local festivals is that they:

choices.A: should avoid pleasure because celebration hides political conflict.
choices.B: prove that communities are unified whenever residents gather publicly.
choices.C: can create shared visibility while also simplifying and excluding.
choices.D: are valuable only when they preserve historical customs unchanged.

explanations.A: The author does not reject pleasure. The strongest festivals leave room for pleasure while admitting tension.
explanations.B: The passage resists treating a crowd as proof of unity. Gatherings can reveal community and flatter it at the same time.
explanations.C: Correct. This captures the central balance. Festivals make a community visible, but their symbols and stories can compress disagreement and hide exclusion.
explanations.D: The passage does not defend unchanged customs. It cares about who can shape public celebration.

choices.A: the physical crowding that makes festivals uncomfortable.
choices.B: the commercial pressure to sell food quickly during public events.
choices.C: the emotional relief produced when conflict is permanently resolved.
choices.D: simplifying complex communal history into celebratory public forms.

explanations.A: Crowding is part of festivals, but the phrase concerns symbolic simplification, not physical density.
explanations.B: Food stalls are mentioned, but commerce is not the meaning of the phrase.
explanations.C: The passage denies that festivals permanently resolve conflict. Their value lies in making shared space possible.
explanations.D: Correct. The passage says festivals choose songs, costumes, foods, and stories that can be repeated easily. That compression is cheerful but potentially concealing.

questions.stem: The examples of official food and historical pageants primarily show that:

choices.A: public rituals can include some groups while leaving others as spectators.
choices.B: communities are strongest when they avoid contested memories.
choices.C: festivals should eliminate all simplified symbols from public celebration.
choices.D: culinary traditions are more politically important than historical narratives.

explanations.A: Correct. The examples show how inclusion can be partial. A group may appear in the festival without having real power to shape it.
explanations.B: The passage argues that disagreement should be able to appear, not be avoided.
explanations.C: The author says simplification is unavoidable, not automatically false.
explanations.D: Food and pageants are parallel examples of selection, not ranked forms of importance.

questions.stem: Which festival would the author most likely praise?

choices.A: One that celebrates while allowing contested histories to be acknowledged.
choices.B: One that celebrates a single heritage while presenting dissent as disloyalty.
choices.C: One that removes all ritual elements to prevent simplification.
choices.D: One that invites minority groups to watch but not help plan the event.

explanations.A: Correct. This matches the passage’s preferred balance: pleasure remains, but disagreement is not treated as a spoiling intrusion.
explanations.B: This is the kind of flattering unity the passage criticizes.
explanations.C: The author does not want to eliminate ritual or pleasure. Shared forms are necessary.
explanations.D: Spectatorship without participation is one of the exclusions the passage identifies.

questions.stem: The author’s attitude toward simplification is best described as:

choices.A: hostile because all simplification falsifies communal life.
choices.B: cautious because simplification is necessary but risky.
choices.C: enthusiastic because simplification creates unity by itself.
choices.D: indifferent because festivals are mainly entertainment.

explanations.A: The passage explicitly says simplification is not automatically false.
explanations.B: Correct. This captures the balanced view. A community cannot gather around every disagreement at once, but selected symbols can hide whose story is missing.
explanations.C: The author warns that simplification can flatter and exclude.
explanations.D: Entertainment is part of the scene, but the passage gives festivals civic significance.

questions.stem: Which finding would most weaken the passage’s concern about festival exclusion?

choices.A: A festival’s official story omits labor conflict from the town’s past.
choices.B: Many residents report feeling proud during the parade.
choices.C: Previously excluded groups now control major symbols and planning.
choices.D: Festival attendance increases when food vendors are placed near the main stage.

explanations.A: This would support the concern that cheerful compression hides conflict.
explanations.B: Pride can coexist with exclusion. The passage is suspicious of pride that proves too easy.
explanations.C: Correct. If formerly marginal groups shape symbols and planning, the passage’s worry about spectator inclusion would be reduced.
explanations.D: Attendance logistics do not address the moral question of who shapes the festival.

questions.stem: The author would most likely agree that belonging at a festival requires:

choices.A: the preservation of every inherited custom without revision.
choices.B: power to shape symbols, not merely appear beside them.
choices.C: the disappearance of disagreement during the event.
choices.D: the replacement of celebration by historical instruction.

explanations.A: The passage is open to revision of public forms.
explanations.B: Correct. The passage emphasizes who has the power to name, cook, route, and narrate the local. Belonging is active participation in public form.
explanations.C: The author wants disagreement to be able to appear without being treated as intrusion.
explanations.D: The author does not oppose celebration; the best festivals keep pleasure and honesty together.
